prompt: In a typical LLM application architecture, which layer is responsible for building the prompt, deciding which model to call, and enforcing rules?
choices: The client (browser/mobile app)
The app / orchestration layer
The vector store
The model provider

prompt: Which of the following are cross-cutting concerns that apply across the whole AI system rather than to a single component? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Keeping secrets and API keys server-side
Privacy and data governance for PII
Reliability and fallbacks when the provider fails
Choosing the exact wording of one specific prompt

prompt: A vector store is included in many architectures primarily to enable which capability?
choices: Retrieving relevant documents to ground the model's answers (RAG)
Storing the model's weights for faster inference
Encrypting the client's network traffic
Billing users per token automatically

prompt: An architect insists the team call the LLM through an internal interface rather than calling the provider's SDK directly throughout the codebase. Which benefits does this provider-abstraction approach provide? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Swapping providers or models becomes a configuration change rather than a rewrite
It makes it easier to add routing (small model for easy tasks, large for hard ones)
It centralizes a single place to add a fallback when the provider fails
It removes the need for any guardrails or output checks

prompt: Coding straight to one provider's SDK ships fastest; a provider-abstraction layer adds work but enables switching later. For an early MVP racing to validate the idea, which *trade-off* is reasonable?
choices: Build a full multi-provider abstraction layer up front so you're never locked in
Hard-code the provider everywhere and never plan for change
Ship directly to one provider now, keeping a thin seam — don't over-engineer abstraction before product-market fit
Support three providers from day one to be safe
